content:
# Acts Chapter 2 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios from Quire 86 (PDF pages 226-227) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_002_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_002_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

### The Day of Pentecost

1 And when the day of Pentecost was being fulfilled, they were all together in one place.

2 And suddenly there came from heaven a sound as of a rushing violent wind, and it filled the whole house where they were sitting.

3 And there appeared to them tongues as of fire, being distributed, and one sat upon each of them.

4 And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues, as the Spirit was giving them utterance.

### The Crowd's Amazement

5 Now there were dwelling in Jerusalem Jews, devout men from every nation under heaven.

6 And when this sound occurred, the multitude came together and was confounded, because each one heard them speaking in his own language.

7 And they were amazed and marveled, saying, "Behold, are not all these who are speaking Galileans?

8 And how is it that we each hear them in our own language in which we were born?

9 Parthians and Medes and Elamites, and those dwelling in Mesopotamia, Judea and Cappadocia, Pontus and Asia,

10 Phrygia and Pamphylia, Egypt and the parts of Libya toward Cyrene, and sojourners from Rome,

11 both Jews and proselytes, Cretans and Arabians — we hear them speaking in our own tongues the mighty works of God."

12 And they were all amazed and perplexed, saying to one another, "What does this mean?"

13 But others, mocking, said, "They are filled with sweet wine."

### Peter's Sermon

14 But Peter, standing with the eleven, lifted up his voice and declared to them, "Men of Judea and all who dwell in Jerusalem, let this be known to you, and give ear to my words.

15 For these men are not drunk, as you suppose, for it is the third hour of the day.

16 But this is what was spoken through the prophet Joel:

17 'And it shall be in the last days, says God,
I will pour out from my Spirit upon all flesh,
and your sons and your daughters shall prophesy,
and your young men shall see visions,
and your elders shall dream dreams;

18 and indeed upon my male servants and upon my female servants
in those days I will pour out from my Spirit,
and they shall prophesy.

19 And I will show wonders in the heaven above
and signs upon the earth below —
blood and fire and vapor of smoke.

20 The sun shall be turned to darkness
and the moon to blood,
before the coming of the great and manifest day of the Lord.

21 And it shall be that everyone who calls upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.'

### Jesus the Nazarene

22 "Men of Israel, hear these words: Jesus the Nazarene, a man attested to you by God with mighty works and wonders and signs, which God did through him in your midst, as you yourselves know —

23 this man, delivered up by the determined counsel and foreknowledge of God, you nailed to a cross by the hands of lawless men and killed.

24 But God raised him up, having loosed the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.

25 For David says concerning him,
'I saw the Lord before me continually,
for he is at my right hand, that I should not be shaken.

26 Therefore my heart was glad and my tongue rejoiced;
moreover my flesh also shall dwell in hope,

27 because you will not abandon my soul to Hades,
nor will you let your Holy One see corruption.

28 You have made known to me the ways of life;
you will fill me with gladness in your presence.'

29 "Brothers, I may speak to you with boldness concerning the patriarch David, that he both died and was buried, and his tomb is among us to this day.

30 Therefore, being a prophet and knowing that God had sworn to him with an oath to seat one from the fruit of his loins upon his throne,

31 he foresaw and spoke concerning the resurrection of the Christ, that he was neither abandoned to Hades, nor did his flesh see corruption.

32 This Jesus God raised up, of which we all are witnesses.

33 Therefore, having been exalted to the right hand of God, and having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this which you both see and hear.

34 For David did not ascend into the heavens, but he himself says,
'The Lord said to my Lord,
"Sit at my right hand,

35 until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet."'

36 Therefore let all the house of Israel know assuredly that God has made him both Lord and Christ — this Jesus whom you crucified."

### The Response of the Crowd

37 Now when they heard this, they were pierced to the heart, and said to Peter and the rest of the apostles, "Brothers, what shall we do?"

38 And Peter said to them, "Repent, and let each of you be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you shall receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.

39 For the promise is to you and to your children, and to all who are far off, as many as the Lord our God shall call to himself."

40 And with many other words he solemnly testified and exhorted them, saying, "Be saved from this crooked generation."

41 So then, those who received his word were baptized, and there were added on that day about three thousand souls.

### The Life of the First Believers

42 And they were continuing steadfastly in the teaching of the apostles and in the fellowship, in the breaking of bread, and in the prayers.

43 And fear came upon every soul, and many wonders and signs were done through the apostles.

44 And all who believed were together and had all things in common,

45 and they were selling their possessions and goods and distributing them to all, as anyone had need.

46 And day by day, continuing steadfastly with one accord in the temple, and breaking bread from house to house, they received their food with gladness and simplicity of heart,

47 praising God and having favor with all the people. And the Lord was adding to their number day by day those who were being saved.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**General agreement:** The three translators show strong alignment across Acts 2. The Pentecost narrative, Peter's sermon, and the community summary are all rendered consistently in substance. Differences are primarily matters of register — EMET preserving Greek word order and morphology, LOGOS rendering into formal English equivalence, and PNEUMA aiming for contemporary clarity. No significant textual-critical divergences from the Sinaiticus reading were observed.

**Key decisions:**

1. **Verse 1 — "being fulfilled" vs. "arrived" (συμπληροῦσθαι):** EMET renders the infinitive literally: "in the being-fulfilled of the day." LOGOS: "was being fulfilled." PNEUMA: "arrived." The Greek συμπληροῦσθαι carries a sense of completion or fullness — the day was "being filled up" or "reaching its fulfillment." This is more theologically weighted than a mere temporal marker. I follow LOGOS's formal rendering "was being fulfilled," which preserves the Greek nuance while reading naturally in English. PNEUMA's "arrived" loses the fulfillment motif that Luke likely intended.

2. **Verse 2 — "rushing violent wind" vs. "violent, rushing wind" (φερομένης πνοῆς βιαίας):** The Greek word order is φερομένης (being-borne/rushing) πνοῆς (wind/breath) βιαίας (violent). EMET: "a violent wind being-borne." LOGOS: "a rushing violent wind." PNEUMA: "a violent, rushing wind." All three are acceptable. I adopt LOGOS's phrasing, which captures both Greek modifiers cleanly.

3. **Verse 6 — "confounded" vs. "confused" vs. "bewildered" (συνεχύθη):** EMET: "was-confused." LOGOS: "was confounded." PNEUMA: "were bewildered." The Greek συγχέω means "to pour together, to confuse, to confound." "Confounded" (LOGOS) best captures the intensity of the Greek — stronger than "confused" or "bewildered." The Sinaiticus reading is clear: ΣΥΝΕΧΥΘΗ.

4. **Verse 13 — "sweet wine" vs. "new wine" (γλεύκους):** EMET: "sweet-wine." LOGOS: "new wine." PNEUMA: "sweet wine." The Greek γλεῦκος refers specifically to sweet, unfermented or partially fermented grape juice — it is not the standard word for wine (οἶνος). "Sweet wine" (EMET, PNEUMA) is more precise. "New wine" (LOGOS) captures the freshness but loses the sweetness nuance. I follow EMET and PNEUMA.

5. **Verse 14 — "Men of Judea" vs. "Men, Jews" vs. "Fellow Jews" (Ἄνδρες Ἰουδαῖοι):** EMET renders hyper-literally: "Men, Jews." LOGOS: "Men of Judea." PNEUMA: "Fellow Jews." The Greek Ἰουδαῖοι can mean either "Jews" (ethnic/religious) or "Judeans" (geographic). In context, Peter addresses both residents and visitors, so "Judea" as purely geographic is slightly narrow. However, "Men of Judea" is a well-established formal rendering and reads well alongside "all who dwell in Jerusalem." I follow LOGOS, noting that PNEUMA's "Fellow Jews" is a legitimate dynamic rendering but loses the formal vocative address.

6. **Verse 20 — "manifest" vs. "glorious" (ἐπιφανῆ):** EMET: "manifest." LOGOS and PNEUMA both condense the Joel quotation. The Greek ἐπιφανῆ (from ἐπιφανής) means "manifest, conspicuous, notable" — related to "epiphany." I adopt "manifest" with EMET, as it preserves the Greek sense of visible, unmistakable appearing without importing "glorious" (which would be ἔνδοξος). Sinaiticus clearly reads ΕΠΙΦΑΝΗ.

7. **Verse 22 — "Nazarene" vs. "Nazorean" vs. "of Nazareth" (Ναζωραῖον):** EMET: "Nazorean." LOGOS: "Nazarene." PNEUMA: "of Nazareth." The Greek form Ναζωραῖος is distinct from Ναζαρηνός (used in Mark). "Nazarene" (LOGOS) is the most widely recognized English form and adequately represents the Greek. EMET's "Nazorean" is more precise but less accessible. PNEUMA's "of Nazareth" is a paraphrase. I follow LOGOS.

8. **Verse 24 — "pangs" vs. "pains" vs. "agony" (ὠδῖνας):** EMET: "pains." LOGOS: "pangs." PNEUMA: "agony." The Greek ὠδίν specifically means "birth-pangs" — the pains of labor. This is theologically rich: death's "labor pains" produced the resurrection, as if death were in travail and could not hold its offspring. "Pangs" (LOGOS) preserves this specialized meaning better than the generic "pains" or "agony." I follow LOGOS.

9. **Verse 33 — "to the right hand" vs. "by the right hand" (τῇ δεξιᾷ):** EMET renders the dative as instrumental: "by the right hand." LOGOS: "to the right hand." PNEUMA: "to God's right hand." The dative τῇ δεξιᾷ can be read as instrumental ("by the right hand") or locative ("at/to the right hand"). The parallel with verse 34 (Psalm 110: "Sit at my right hand") and the context of exaltation favor the locative reading — Christ is exalted *to* God's right hand, not exalted *by means of* a right hand. I follow LOGOS and PNEUMA.

10. **Verse 36 — word order and emphasis:** EMET places "both Lord and Christ" before "God made him," following the Greek predicate-first order. LOGOS rearranges for English clarity: "God has made him both Lord and Christ." PNEUMA dramatically fronts "this Jesus": "God has made this Jesus... both Lord and Messiah." The Greek places ΚΑΙ ΚΥΡΙΟΝ ΑΥΤΟΝ ΚΑΙ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΝ ΕΠΟΙΗΣΕΝ Ο ΘΕΟΣ — the emphatic position is on "both Lord and Christ." I follow LOGOS's English arrangement but retain the emphatic appositive "this Jesus whom you crucified" at the end, as in the Greek.

11. **Verse 38 — "in the name" vs. "upon the name" (ἐπὶ τῷ ὀνόματι):** EMET: "upon the name." LOGOS: "in the name." PNEUMA: "in the name." The Greek preposition is ἐπί with the dative, literally "upon the name." Most English translations render this "in the name," which has become the standard baptismal formula in English. I follow the majority rendering "in the name" with LOGOS and PNEUMA, while noting that EMET's "upon" is technically more precise to the preposition.

12. **Verse 40 — "crooked" vs. "corrupt" (σκολιᾶς):** EMET and LOGOS: "crooked." PNEUMA: "corrupt." The Greek σκολιός literally means "crooked, bent, curved" (English "scoliosis" derives from it). It is used in the LXX of Deuteronomy 32:5 for a morally perverse generation. "Crooked" preserves the Greek metaphor; "corrupt" is interpretive. I follow EMET and LOGOS.

13. **Verse 41 — "received his word" vs. "embraced his message" (ἀποδεξάμενοι τὸν λόγον):** EMET: "having-received the word of-him." LOGOS: "received his word." PNEUMA: "embraced his message." The Greek ἀποδέχομαι means "to receive, accept, welcome." LOGOS strikes the right balance. PNEUMA's "embraced" adds warmth not in the Greek; "message" for λόγος is acceptable but "word" is more standard. I follow LOGOS.

14. **Verse 42 — "continuing steadfastly" vs. "devoted themselves" (προσκαρτεροῦντες):** EMET: "persevering." LOGOS: "continuing steadfastly." PNEUMA: "devoted themselves." The Greek προσκαρτερέω means "to persist in, to hold fast to, to continue steadfastly." LOGOS's rendering preserves the durative, ongoing quality of the participle. PNEUMA's "devoted themselves" is strong but slightly punctiliar. I follow LOGOS.

15. **Verse 46 — "simplicity" vs. "sincerity" (ἀφελότητι):** EMET and LOGOS: "simplicity." PNEUMA: "sincere." The Greek ἀφελότης means "simplicity, singleness" — an absence of pretense or duplicity. "Simplicity" is the more direct rendering. I follow EMET and LOGOS.

16. **Verse 47 — "to their number" vs. "upon the same [place]" (ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτό):** EMET: "upon the same [place]." LOGOS: "to their number." PNEUMA: "to their number." The Greek phrase ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτό is ambiguous — it can mean "together, in the same place" or can function as "to the [community]." In Sinaiticus, the phrase ΕΠΙ ΤΟ ΑΥΤΟ appears at the end of the verse. Many manuscripts and translations take it with the beginning of 3:1 rather than the end of 2:47. I follow LOGOS and PNEUMA in reading it with 2:47 as "to their number," which yields smoother English and reflects the sense of communal addition.

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 1 — συμπληροῦσθαι: "being fulfilled" or "arrived"?

**The variant:** The infinitive συμπληροῦσθαι carries fulfillment language that may be theologically significant or merely temporal.

- **EMET** rendered "in the being-fulfilled of the day" (preserving the fullness/completion sense).
- **LOGOS** rendered "when the day of Pentecost was being fulfilled" (same sense, smoother English).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "when the day of Pentecost arrived" (purely temporal).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS. Luke uses συμπληρόω deliberately (cf. Luke 9:51, where the same verb describes the "days being fulfilled" for Jesus's ascension to Jerusalem). This is fulfillment language, not mere chronology. PNEUMA's "arrived" is natural English but erases a Lukan motif.

### Verse 13 — γλεύκους: "sweet wine" or "new wine"?

**The variant:** The Greek γλεῦκος refers to a specific type of wine.

- **EMET** rendered "sweet-wine."
- **LOGOS** rendered "new wine."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "sweet wine."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET and PNEUMA with "sweet wine." The Greek γλεῦκος specifically denotes sweet, freshly pressed grape juice that could be mildly intoxicating. "New wine" (LOGOS) could be confused with νέος οἶνος (new wine in new wineskins, Matt 9:17), which is a different term. "Sweet wine" preserves the distinction.

### Verse 14 — Ἄνδρες Ἰουδαῖοι: "Men of Judea" or "Men, Jews" or "Fellow Jews"?

**The variant:** The vocative address Ἄνδρες Ἰουδαῖοι is rendered differently across the three translations.

- **EMET** rendered "Men, Jews" (treating Ἰουδαῖοι as a noun in apposition).
- **LOGOS** rendered "Men of Judea" (geographic reading).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "Fellow Jews" (dynamic equivalent).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "Men of Judea." While Ἰουδαῖοι can be ethnic or geographic, the formal address pattern Ἄνδρες + ethnic/geographic identifier (cf. v.22 Ἄνδρες Ἰσραηλῖται, "Men of Israel") is a Lukan rhetorical formula. "Men of Judea" maintains the formal oratorical register appropriate to Peter's public sermon. PNEUMA's "Fellow Jews" is too casual for the elevated rhetoric.

### Verse 24 — ὠδῖνας: "pangs" or "pains" or "agony"?

**The variant:** The noun ὠδίν carries specific connotations of birth-labor.

- **EMET** rendered "pains of death."
- **LOGOS** rendered "pangs of death."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "agony of death."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "pangs of death." The Greek ὠδίν is the technical term for labor pains (birth pangs). This may echo the LXX of Psalm 18:5 (=17:5 LXX) where "cords/pangs of death" entangled the psalmist. "Pangs" in English retains the birth-pain connotation (as in "pangs of childbirth"). EMET's "pains" is too generic; PNEUMA's "agony" imports intensity not necessarily present in the Greek term.

### Verse 33 — τῇ δεξιᾷ: Instrumental or locative dative?

**The variant:** The dative τῇ δεξιᾷ τοῦ θεοῦ can be parsed as instrumental ("by the right hand of God") or locative ("to/at the right hand of God").

- **EMET** rendered "by the right hand of God" (instrumental).
- **LOGOS** rendered "to the right hand of God" (locative).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "to God's right hand" (locative).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows the locative reading with LOGOS and PNEUMA: "to the right hand of God." The immediate context favors this: (a) the verb ὑψωθείς ("having been exalted") naturally takes a directional complement; (b) verse 34 quotes Psalm 110:1, "Sit at my right hand," establishing the right hand as the *location* of exaltation; (c) Peter's argument builds toward the conclusion that Jesus now occupies the position of authority at God's right hand. The instrumental reading ("by means of God's right hand") is grammatically possible but contextually weaker.

### Verse 38 — ἐπὶ τῷ ὀνόματι: "in the name" or "upon the name"?

**The variant:** The preposition ἐπί with the dative is rendered differently.

- **EMET** rendered "upon the name of Jesus Christ" (literal).
- **LOGOS** rendered "in the name of Jesus Christ" (standard English baptismal formula).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "in the name of Jesus the Messiah" (dynamic, with "Messiah" for Χριστός).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "in the name of Jesus Christ." While EMET's "upon" more precisely represents ἐπί, "in the name" is the established English rendering for this baptismal formula and does not distort the meaning. Regarding "Christ" vs. "Messiah" — PNEUMA's choice to render Χριστός as "Messiah" is linguistically valid (Χριστός = anointed one = Hebrew Mashiach), but in Koine Greek, Χριστός had already become a quasi-proper name in early Christian usage. I retain "Christ" with EMET and LOGOS.

### Verse 47 — ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτό: Placement and meaning

**The variant:** The phrase ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτό at the end of verse 47 is ambiguous in both meaning and verse assignment.

- **EMET** rendered "upon the same [place]" (spatial, attached to 2:47).
- **LOGOS** rendered "to their number" (communal addition, attached to 2:47).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "to their number" (same as LOGOS).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS and PNEUMA in reading ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτό as "to their number" and attaching it to 2:47. In Sinaiticus, the phrase appears at the end of the Acts 2 text block. While some traditions connect it to 3:1 ("Peter and John were going up to the temple together"), the phrase functions as a summary conclusion to the community description: the Lord was adding the saved "to the same [group]," i.e., to their number. This reading provides a strong closure to the Pentecost narrative.

---

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
